name: Haumia
description: Haumia, also known as Haumia-tiketike, is a significant deity in Māori mythology, primarily associated with uncultivated or wild foods, particularly fernroot (aruhe). He is one of the children of Ranginui (sky father) and Papatūānuku (earth mother). When Tāne Mahuta separated his parents, Ranginui and Papatūānuku, forcing light to enter the world, Haumia, along with his brothers, faced immense upheaval. He sought refuge underground to escape the light and the wrath of his brothers, particularly Tāwhirimātea, the god of storms, who was furious at the separation of their parents. Hiding deep within the earth, Haumia became the guardian and embodiment of the wild, uncultivated foods that grow beneath the surface. He represents sustenance that is not directly cultivated by humans but is provided naturally by the earth. His power lies in his ability to conceal himself and the foods he protects, making them available when needed. While often depicted as a passive figure compared to his more aggressive brothers, Haumia's role is crucial in ensuring the survival and well-being of humankind by providing a vital food source. He is deeply connected to the earth and its bounty, representing the hidden resources that sustain life. He also embodies resilience and the ability to endure hardship by seeking refuge and adapting to challenging circumstances.
subtitle: God of wild, uncultivated foods.

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Rangi and Papa
keyMyths.description: Haumia hid underground with his brother Rongo when Tāne separated their parents. He became the guardian of wild foods.

keyMyths.title: Haumia's Hiding Place
keyMyths.description: To avoid the wrath of Tāwhirimātea, Haumia burrowed deep into the earth, concealing himself and the fernroot.
keyMyths.source: Reed, A. W. (2002). The Reed Dictionary of Māori Mythology.

keyMyths.title: The Source of Sustenance
keyMyths.description: Haumia provides sustenance through the wild foods he protects, offering a vital resource for survival in the natural world.
keyMyths.source: Orbell, Margaret. The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Māori Mythology.

sources.author: Grey, Sir George
sources.description: A classic compilation of Māori myths and legends.
sources.text: A classic compilation of Māori myths and legends.

sources.author: Reed, A. W.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings of Fernroot
worshipPractices.description: Traditional offerings of fernroot and other wild foods were made to Haumia to ensure a plentiful harvest and to acknowledge his role in providing sustenance. These offerings were often accompanied by karakia (prayers) beseeching his favor.

worshipPractices.name: Respect for the Land
worshipPractices.description: Showing respect for the land and its natural resources was a form of honoring Haumia. This included sustainable harvesting practices and avoiding unnecessary destruction of wild food sources.

festivals.name: Harvest Celebrations
festivals.description: While not exclusively dedicated to Haumia, harvest festivals often included acknowledgements of his role in providing wild foods. These celebrations marked the end of the growing season and the beginning of a time of feasting and sharing.

cultural.modernLegacy: Haumia's legacy persists in contemporary Māori culture through the continued importance of traditional food gathering practices and a renewed emphasis on environmental stewardship. His name and stories serve as reminders of the interconnectedness between people and the natural world.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: In Māori cosmology, Haumia-tiketike is one of the offspring of Ranginui (the sky father) and Papatūānuku (the earth mother). Initially, their children lived in the darkness between their parents, who were locked in a tight embrace. Tāne Mahuta, god of the forest, eventually separated them, allowing light to enter the world. Haumia, along with his brothers, vehemently opposed this separation, as it disrupted the primordial unity they had always known. Following the separation, Tāwhirimātea, the god of storms, unleashed his fury upon his siblings in retaliation for their act of separating their parents. Haumia, to escape Tāwhirimātea's wrath, sought refuge within the bosom of Papatūānuku, his earth mother, where he found protection and concealment. This act symbolizes his deep connection to the earth and its resources, particularly those that grow wild and untended.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth involving Haumia revolves around his role in providing sustenance to humanity and his conflict with his brother Rongomātāne, the god of cultivated plants. When Tāwhirimātea attacked his brothers, Haumia hid within the earth, becoming the guardian of wild foods, especially fernroot. In some narratives, Rongomātāne represents the opposing force, the cultivated foods that require human intervention. The ongoing tension between Haumia and Rongomātāne reflects the balance between relying on nature's bounty and cultivating one's own food sources. This dynamic also highlights the Māori understanding of resource management and the importance of respecting both wild and cultivated environments. The stories emphasize the resourcefulness and adaptability required for survival in a challenging environment.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Formal worship of Haumia, in the sense of elaborate temples or organized priesthoods, was not a prominent feature of Māori religious practice. Instead, reverence for Haumia was expressed through respect for the land and its resources. Offerings of fernroot and other wild foods were sometimes made to acknowledge his role in providing sustenance. Karakia (prayers or incantations) were recited to ensure a plentiful harvest of wild foods and to seek Haumia's blessing. The act of gathering wild foods itself was often imbued with a sense of spiritual significance, recognizing the gift of nature and the importance of sustainable harvesting practices. This approach emphasizes the practical and reciprocal relationship between humans and the natural world, reflecting a deep understanding of ecological balance.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Haumia's influence extends beyond his role as the god of uncultivated foods. He represents the resilience and resourcefulness of the Māori people in adapting to their environment. His association with fernroot, a staple food source in traditional Māori diet, underscores the importance of utilizing available resources sustainably. In contemporary Māori culture, Haumia serves as a reminder of the interconnectedness between people and the natural world. His stories promote environmental awareness and encourage responsible stewardship of the land. The increasing interest in traditional Māori knowledge and practices has led to a renewed appreciation for Haumia and his significance in preserving cultural heritage and promoting ecological sustainability. He embodies the principle of Kaitiakitanga, guardianship and protection of the environment for future generations.

powers: Control over wild and uncultivated plants
Ability to conceal himself within the earth
Connection to the earth and its hidden resources
Knowledge of medicinal properties of plants
shortDescription: Haumia, also known as Haumia-tiketike, is the Māori deity of uncultivated foods, particularly fernroot, and is a child of Ranginui and Papatūānuku.
